title: Protestele din Iran – există speranța unui stat civil și a întoarcerii la democrație
summary: The article traces Iran's democratic traditions from the 1905 Constitutional Revolution through the Mosaddegh era, arguing that the 1953 CIA-MI6 coup destroyed secular opposition and paved the way for theocracy. The author watches current Iranian protests with hope that the country might recover its interrupted constitutional and parliamentary heritage rather than settle for monarchy or softened Islamism.
talking_points: Iran had a genuine constitutional and parliamentary tradition dating back to 1905-1911, with rule of law concepts, civil rights, and separation of powers
PM Mosaddegh's democratic government was overthrown in 1953 by CIA and MI6 primarily to control Iranian oil, replacing democracy with authoritarian monarchy
The Shah's secret police SAVAK destroyed secular and democratic opposition, leaving religious clergy as the only organized opposition force
The 1979 Islamic Revolution was an ironic consequence of Western intervention that eliminated all non-religious opposition
Current protests represent hope for recovering Iran's pre-1953 democratic traditions rather than accepting monarchy or reformed Islamism
author: Doina Popescu
